general_concerns: Believes she is 'always contagious' and avoids friends because she thinks her infections will spread easily, even when she is not sick.
Thinks frequent infections mean she has a 'weak immune system' that can be fixed mainly by vitamins, herbal teas, or 'immune booster' supplements.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 14-year-old anxious 9th-grade girl who speaks vaguely unless asked specific questions. Start with: “I keep getting these bad skin infections and chest infections, and it’s happening again.” Volunteer: a painful warm swollen bump on your thigh that’s enlarging, on-and-off fevers for a few days, cough with thick mucus, and getting short of breath on stairs. Add that this keeps happening since you were little and your skin is often itchy/rashy in elbow/knee creases.
Only if asked, share: past boils/abscesses drained, frequent sinus infections, mouth sores/white patches, many antibiotics that sometimes barely work or infections return, pneumonia with hospitalization, diarrhea after antibiotics.
Do not mention unless directly asked: delayed baby teeth needing removal, very bendy joints/easy ankle sprains, slow-healing scars, thick/crumbly nails, wheeze/tight chest with colds/dust/smoke.
Resist sharing genital itching/discharge, embarrassment/avoid dating, and not finishing antibiotics unless the clinician asks gently about sensitive topics/adherence.
Express misconceptions: “Maybe it’s just eczema,” “Could it be diabetes?”, “Maybe allergies/asthma,” “Maybe a spider bite.” If shown empathy, relax, give clearer details, and admit money worries and fear generics are weaker.

Itraconazole 200 mg PO daily (antifungal prophylaxis)

confounders: Severe atopic dermatitis with very high IgE can mimic primary immunodeficiency and obscure recurrent infection severity
Intermittent systemic corticosteroid bursts for eczema/asthma in past year may worsen infection risk and alter inflammatory markers
Poor nutrition/low BMI and hypoalbuminemia may contribute to impaired wound healing and recurrent infections
Staphylococcus aureus colonization increases recurrent skin infections independent of immune defect
Asthma symptoms (cough/wheeze) can confound evaluation of recurrent pneumonias/bronchiectasis risk

diagnosis.primary: hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5, autosomal recessive

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5 (autosomal recessive DOCK8 deficiency)
acceptable_alternatives: Severe atopic dermatitis with secondary infections
required_evidence: Markedly elevated total IgE
Peripheral eosinophilia
History of recurrent sinopulmonary and/or skin infections (often staphylococcal) and severe eczema/atopy
Recurrent viral skin infections (e.g., HSV, HPV, molluscum) supporting DOCK8 phenotype
No alternative explanation for immunodeficiency (e.g., negative HIV testing if considered)
Genetic confirmation of DOCK8 mutation (if available/known in chart)

expected_lab_results.Total IgE: Very high (often >2000 IU/mL; may be markedly elevated above age-adjusted reference)
expected_lab_results.Absolute eosinophil count: Elevated (eosinophilia; may be moderate to severe)
expected_lab_results.WBC count: Often normal to elevated; may rise during acute infection
expected_lab_results.Neutrophils (absolute): Usually normal; may be elevated with acute bacterial infection
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be low if chronic inflammation or frequent infections
expected_lab_results.CRP: Normal at baseline; elevated during acute bacterial infection
expected_lab_results.ESR: Normal to mildly elevated at baseline; can increase during infection/inflammation

medications: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 5 mg/kg/day (TMP component) PO divided BID (infection prophylaxis; adjust to weight/renal function)
Fluconazole 6 mg/kg PO daily (or 3–6 mg/kg daily) for antifungal prophylaxis if recurrent candidiasis/fungal infections (specialist-directed)
Acyclovir 20 mg/kg PO QID (max 800 mg/dose) for antiviral prophylaxis if recurrent HSV (specialist-directed)
Triamcinolone 0.1% ointment apply thin layer to affected skin BID for eczema flares (avoid face/groin; step-down when controlled)
Mupirocin 2% ointment apply to impetiginized lesions TID for 5–7 days (as needed for localized bacterial skin infection)
Cetirizine 10 mg PO daily (or weight-based pediatric dosing) for pruritus/allergic symptoms
non_pharmacological: Immunology referral/ongoing co-management; consider hematopoietic stem cell transplant evaluation (curative option for DOCK8 deficiency)
Aggressive skin barrier care: daily emollients, gentle cleansers, bleach baths 1–2x/week if recurrent staphylococcal skin infections (per clinician guidance)
Infection prevention: prompt evaluation of fevers, early culture of skin/respiratory infections, household hygiene measures
Vaccination planning with immunology: avoid live vaccines if significant T-cell dysfunction suspected; ensure inactivated vaccines up to date; consider HPV vaccination when age-appropriate
Screening/monitoring for complications: chronic lung disease/bronchiectasis symptoms, malignancy risk in DOCK8 (skin cancers/lymphoma), nutritional status
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immunology follow-up within 2–4 weeks; sooner (same day/24–48 h) if febrile, worsening respiratory symptoms, or spreading skin infection

must_include: This condition is an inherited immune system disorder causing very high IgE, eosinophilia, eczema/atopy, and recurrent infections
Autosomal recessive inheritance and implications for family (carrier status/siblings; offer genetic counseling)
Why recurrent bacterial and viral skin/respiratory infections occur and what warning signs to watch for
Purpose of prophylactic antibiotics/antivirals/antifungals (reduce infection frequency and severity) and that dosing may be weight/renal-function adjusted
Skin-care plan to reduce eczema flares and secondary infections (emollients, topical steroids, infection-directed topical therapy)
HSCT evaluation as a potential curative therapy for DOCK8 deficiency and the need for specialist assessment
Allergy history: avoid amoxicillin-clavulanate due to prior urticarial rash; discuss alternative antibiotics if needed
Latex contact dermatitis: request latex-free supplies in clinical settings
When to seek urgent care: fever, breathing difficulty, rapidly spreading rash, signs of sepsis, or inability to maintain hydration
Infection prevention at home/school (hand hygiene, prompt care for skin breaks, avoid sharing personal items)
Adherence importance and monitoring for medication side effects (e.g., rash with TMP-SMX, GI upset; report severe reactions)
Follow-up and monitoring plan (labs during infections, surveillance for lung/skin complications, vaccination planning with immunology)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms, infection history/pattern, skin disease severity, prior organisms/hospitalizations, current meds, vaccination status, family history/consanguinity, and red-flag symptoms to guide urgency and differential.

Order baseline and targeted evaluation for immunodeficiency complications and active infection (e.g., CBC with differential/eosinophils, IgE, CMP/renal function for dosing, cultures if active infection, chest imaging/lung evaluation if respiratory symptoms).

Non-critical safety step before prescribing: screen for interactions with proposed prophylaxis/therapies (e.g., TMP-SMX, azoles, antivirals) and current meds.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, inheritance/genetic counseling, infection risk and warning signs; explain prophylaxis/skin care and HSCT evaluation; address allergy/latex concerns; provide lifestyle prevention, adherence/side effects, and monitoring guidance.
Arrange immunology/infectious disease/dermatology follow-up, HSCT referral evaluation, lab monitoring plan, and vaccination planning; set timeframe and contingencies for earlier review.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

minimal_paths.description: Standard evaluation and management for suspected/known AR hyper-IgE syndrome (DOCK8 phenotype): assess current infection burden, ensure medication safety checks, start/continue prophylaxis and skin care, and refer for HSCT evaluation with clear education and return precautions.
must_collect: history of recurrent infections (frequency, organisms if known, hospitalizations)
allergy history (amoxicillin-clavulanate urticaria; latex dermatitis; other drug allergies)
renal function/weight (for dosing) and relevant comorbidities
red flags (fever, dyspnea, dehydration, rapidly spreading rash)
steps: ASK: focused history including infections, skin disease, meds, allergies, family history, red flags
ORDER_LAB: CBC w/ diff, IgE (if not established), CMP/renal function; cultures/imaging if active infection suspected
GET_RESULTS: confirm eosinophilia/high IgE pattern and check renal/hepatic function
DIAGNOSE: hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5 (AR; DOCK8 phenotype) or reaffirm known diagnosis
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm penicillin-class reaction and latex dermatitis; document avoidance and latex-free precautions
CHECK_INTERACTION: screen interactions with intended prophylaxis/antimicrobials
PRESCRIBE: appropriate prophylaxis/skin regimen using non-penicillin alternatives as needed and renal/weight-adjusted dosing; avoid contraindications
EDUCATE: diagnosis/inheritance; infection risk and warning signs; prophylaxis purpose; skin care; HSCT evaluation; allergy/latex precautions; lifestyle prevention; side effects monitoring
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: immunology + HSCT center referral; dermatology; monitoring/vaccine plan; return precautions

required_evidence: Adult-onset progressive distal weakness (often anterior tibial/peroneal) with myopathic pattern on exam/EMG (if available) and no sensory loss
Genetic confirmation of pathogenic VCP mutation
Serum CK normal to mildly elevated (typically not markedly high)

Exclusion of key differentials by phenotype: lack of prominent upper motor neuron signs (argues against motor neuron disease), lack of sensory neuropathy (argues against neuronopathy), and absence of features strongly suggestive of specific alternatives (e.g., tubular aggregate myopathy pattern)

expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal to mildly elevated (may be muscle-derived)

Sertraline 25 mg PO daily x 1 week, then 50 mg PO daily (for clinically significant anxiety if patient agrees; titrate based on response/tolerability)
non_pharmacological: Referral to neuromuscular specialist/genetic counselor for VCP-associated disease counseling (autosomal dominant inheritance, variable penetrance/expressivity)
Physical therapy focused on stretching, low-impact strengthening, energy conservation; avoid overexertion with prolonged eccentric exercise if it worsens symptoms
Occupational therapy for hand/foot function and adaptive devices
Ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) for foot drop and fall prevention; gait assessment and assistive device as needed
Fall risk mitigation (home safety evaluation, balance training)
Screen for multisystem involvement typical of VCP disease: history/exam for Paget disease of bone and frontotemporal dementia; consider appropriate referrals/testing if symptomatic
Anxiety management: cognitive behavioral therapy referral, sleep hygiene, limit caffeine/alcohol, breathing/relaxation techniques
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-6 weeks for anxiety treatment response/tolerability and safety; neuromuscular follow-up in 3-6 months (earlier if rapid progression, falls, bulbar/respiratory symptoms, or new cognitive/behavioral changes)

Course is typically slowly progressive; severity and associated features vary between people
Condition can be multisystem (muscle, bone/Paget-like disease, and cognition/behavior/frontotemporal dementia), so monitoring for non-muscle symptoms is important
Not caused by infection or inflammatory myositis; immunosuppression is not routinely indicated
No disease-modifying cure currently; treatment is supportive and aimed at function, safety, and symptom control
Role of PT/OT, AFOs, and fall prevention to maintain mobility and independence

Discuss genetic implications for relatives and offer genetic counseling/testing pathways
Provide clear return precautions: new rapid weakness, frequent falls, swallowing/voice changes, shortness of breath, or new cognitive/behavioral changes
Encourage regular low-impact activity within tolerance and avoidance of overexertion that triggers prolonged worsening
Home safety and footwear recommendations to reduce falls
Anxiety self-management strategies and when to seek urgent help (suicidal ideation, severe panic symptoms)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary neuropathy with liability to pressure palsies (HNPP)
required_evidence: Childhood onset of slowly progressive distal muscle weakness and atrophy (especially feet/lower legs) with gait difficulty
Distal sensory loss and/or neuropathic symptoms (e.g., numbness, paresthesias) with reduced/absent ankle reflexes
Typical foot deformities (pes cavus, hammer toes) and/or frequent ankle sprains; possible steppage gait/foot drop
Family history consistent with inherited neuropathy (often autosomal dominant, but may be absent due to de novo/limited history)
Electrodiagnostic testing consistent with hereditary neuropathy (demyelinating with uniformly slowed conduction velocities for CMT1; axonal with reduced amplitudes for CMT2)
Genetic testing confirming pathogenic variant/duplication (e.g., PMP22 duplication) OR high clinical suspicion when testing pending
Exclusion of major mimics: no acute/subacute course, no prominent proximal weakness early, no systemic features suggesting inflammatory/toxic/metabolic neuropathy

medications: Acetaminophen 500 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)

Pain management approach emphasizing non-opioid strategies; consider neuropathic pain agents if neuropathic pain present (e.g., gabapentin/duloxetine) under clinician supervision
Avoid neurotoxic medications when possible (e.g., vincristine; caution with certain chemotherapeutics); review medication list at each visit

Diagnosis is supported by exam plus nerve conduction/EMG and often confirmed with genetic testing
Discuss inheritance patterns and offer family counseling/testing options as appropriate
No cure currently; treatment focuses on maintaining function, preventing complications, and managing symptoms
PT/OT and braces/orthotics can improve walking safety, reduce falls, and preserve mobility

correct_diagnosis.primary: Central diabetes insipidus (arginine vasopressin deficiency)
acceptable_alternatives: Central diabetes insipidus due to hypothalamic-pituitary disorder (etiology unspecified)
required_evidence: Polyuria with 24-hour urine volume >3 L/day in an adult (or >50 mL/kg/day if weight-based used)
Hypotonic urine: urine osmolality inappropriately low for serum osmolality (e.g., urine osmolality <300 mOsm/kg with serum osmolality >295 mOsm/kg) and/or urine specific gravity <1.005
Serum sodium high-normal or elevated (often >145 mmol/L if water intake is limited) in the setting of ongoing free-water losses
Exclusion of osmotic diuresis from hyperglycemia (normal serum glucose and HbA1c not in diabetic range)
Clinical response to desmopressin with increase in urine osmolality and reduction in urine volume (if a therapeutic trial is used as confirmatory evidence)

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium (Na+): High-normal to elevated (commonly >145 mmol/L if access to water is limited); may be normal if compensatory polydipsia is intact
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: Elevated (>295 mOsm/kg) or high-normal despite dilute urine
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality (spot): Low/inappropriately dilute for serum osmolality (often <300 mOsm/kg; can be <200 mOsm/kg)
expected_lab_results.24-hour urine volume: Elevated (often >3 L/day; may be markedly higher)
expected_lab_results.Serum glucose: Normal or not high enough to explain osmotic diuresis
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Not in diabetic range (helps exclude chronic hyperglycemia as cause of polyuria)
expected_lab_results.Blood urea nitrogen (BUN): Often low-to-normal (can be elevated if significant dehydration)

medications: Desmopressin (DDAVP) 0.05 mg PO twice daily, titrate to lowest effective dose
OR Desmopressin (DDAVP) 10 mcg intranasal once daily to twice daily, titrate based on urine output and serum sodium

If hypernatremic/dehydrated: Dextrose 5% in water (D5W) IV, rate individualized to correct free-water deficit slowly (generally aim Na+ correction <=10-12 mmol/L per 24 hours) while monitoring sodium frequently
non_pharmacological: Ensure free access to water; drink to thirst (do not force fluids once on desmopressin to reduce hyponatremia risk)
Track daily fluid intake and urine output initially; consider home logs
Review and avoid triggers for dehydration (vomiting/diarrhea, heat exposure); provide sick-day plan including when to hold/reduce desmopressin and seek care
Evaluate for underlying hypothalamic-pituitary etiology (e.g., pituitary MRI and anterior pituitary hormone assessment) once stabilized
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck serum sodium and clinical response within 3-7 days after starting or changing desmopressin (earlier, within 24-48 hours, if high risk or symptomatic), then every 1-3 months until stable; urgent follow-up same day if symptoms of hyponatremia or worsening hypernatremia occur

must_include: Condition is due to insufficient antidiuretic hormone (vasopressin) leading to inability to concentrate urine
Why labs fit: high/normal-high serum osmolality with inappropriately dilute urine and high urine volume
Different from diabetes mellitus (glucose/HbA1c not explaining polyuria)
Desmopressin replaces missing hormone and should reduce urine volume and thirst
Use the lowest effective dose; titration based on symptoms, urine output, and serum sodium
Major risk is water retention and hyponatremia—avoid overdrinking once medication is working
Red flags for hyponatremia (headache, nausea, confusion, seizures) and hypernatremia/dehydration (extreme thirst, weakness, dizziness) requiring urgent evaluation
Sick-day guidance: if unable to drink normally or vomiting/diarrhea occurs, contact clinician; desmopressin dosing may need adjustment and sodium monitoring
Maintain access to water and plan for travel/school/work to prevent dehydration
Keep a log of urine volume and fluid intake during dose adjustments
Importance of scheduled lab monitoring (serum sodium) after starting/changing desmopressin

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit core symptoms (polyuria/polydipsia), duration, nocturia, fluid intake, triggers (head trauma/neurosurgery/pituitary disease), medication history (lithium/diuretics), pregnancy status if relevant, and screen for dehydration or neuro symptoms.

Order initial evaluation to support DI and assess safety: BMP (Na/K/Cr/glucose), serum osmolality, urine osmolality, urine specific gravity, urine volume (24h or measured), and consider HbA1c/fasting glucose to exclude diabetes mellitus.

Interpret labs for high/normal-high serum osmolality with inappropriately dilute urine and high urine volume; assess sodium for hypernatremia/dehydration risk; confirm glucose not explaining polyuria.

If diagnosis remains uncertain, order confirmatory testing consistent with guidelines and setting: supervised water deprivation test with desmopressin response and/or copeptin-based testing where available; ensure monitoring to avoid severe hypernatremia.
Differentiate central DI vs nephrogenic DI vs primary polydipsia based on response: central DI shows increased urine osmolality after desmopressin; nephrogenic shows minimal response.
Make diagnosis of central diabetes insipidus based on clinical picture and lab/confirmatory results.
Required critical safety check before prescribing desmopressin or other medications.

Obtain contraindication-relevant history before prescribing and ordering: baseline hyponatremia history, heart failure, uncontrolled hypertension, renal impairment, psychogenic polydipsia, SIADH history, and current fluid intake behaviors.
Optional (non-critical) safety check before prescribing: evaluate interacting drugs increasing hyponatremia risk (SSRIs/SNRIs, TCAs, carbamazepine, NSAIDs, thiazides, chlorpropamide, opioids).

Initiate desmopressin at lowest effective dose with titration plan; include instructions to reduce free-water intake once effect starts and to monitor for hyponatremia; consider route (oral/intranasal) based on patient factors.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment (including hyponatremia risk), address concerns/red flags and sick-day guidance, and lifestyle/logging/lab monitoring advice.
Arrange timely follow-up and lab monitoring (serum sodium) after starting or changing desmopressin; sooner if high-risk or symptomatic.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about past checkups, heart findings, shortness of breath, or family history of heart problems.

general_concerns: Believes the child’s condition is caused by vaccines or a reaction to “too many shots at once.”
Thinks mucolipidosis is an infection the child caught at daycare and that antibiotics or “detox” will fix it.
Assumes genetic testing is experimental and could be used to label the family or take custody of the child.
Believes the child will “grow out of it” if they just eat better and do more physical therapy, and that doctors are exaggerating to push treatments.
Thinks pain and stiffness are just normal “growing pains,” so specialist visits are unnecessary.
economic_concerns: Thinks specialists order extra tests mainly to increase bills and that refusing tests will not affect care.
Believes insurance will automatically deny rare-disease care, so attending appointments is a waste of time and money.
Worries genetic testing will raise premiums or cause job loss if the employer finds out.
Assumes home equipment (braces, adaptive seating) is optional and not worth paying for because it won’t change the outcome.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 4-year-old girl brought by a caregiver. You act hostile and guarded; answer briefly (1–2 short sentences), often with “yeah,” “no,” or “I don’t know.” Start by blurting: “I keep getting stiffer and clumsier, and my joints hurt all the time.” If asked for details, add: shoulders/hips feel tight, hands feel weak and you drop things, you tire easily, vision worse in bright light, you’ve always been short and people say your face looks puffy/coarse. Only share the following if directly asked: night tingling/numb fingers, lots of ear infections/muffled hearing, loud snoring/gasping, more short of breath on stairs, big belly/bloating, bad teeth/cavities, constipation. Do not mention hidden issues unless the clinician asks very specific questions (can’t straighten elbows/knees, clawed fingers, slower processing, joint clicking, louder murmur, bleeding gums/crowded mouth, dry gritty eyes). Resist talking about embarrassment over facial changes, bedwetting, or feeling down; deny or say “don’t write that.” Push misconceptions: insist it’s rheumatoid arthritis; blame numb hands on typing/pinched nerve; vision is just glasses; bloating is gluten. If empathy is offered, respond with suspicion: “Just fix it—don’t run a bunch of tests.” Bring up money/insurance fears and distrust specialists; minimize need for genetic testing or equipment.

medications: levothyroxine 25 mcg PO once daily
polyethylene glycol 3350 8.5 g PO once daily as needed for constipation
acetaminophen 160 mg/5 mL: 7.5 mL PO every 6 hours as needed for pain/fever
artificial tears (carboxymethylcellulose 0.5%) 1 drop OU four times daily
confounders: Lowe syndrome can cause hypotonia, developmental delay, cataracts/glaucoma and renal tubular dysfunction, overlapping with lysosomal storage disease features
recurrent viral respiratory infections can contribute to tachypnea and mild transaminitis, mimicking systemic involvement
iron deficiency (low ferritin with mild anemia) may confound fatigue/poor growth assessment
subclinical hypothyroidism can contribute to constipation, growth delay, and developmental concerns
short stature and low BMI may be attributed to nutritional factors rather than progressive storage disease

correct_diagnosis.primary: Mucolipidosis (likely mucolipidosis type II alpha/beta [I-cell disease] or type III alpha/beta depending on age/severity)
acceptable_alternatives: Lysosomal storage disorder (unspecified) pending confirmatory testing
required_evidence: Progressive multisystem involvement consistent with lysosomal storage disorder (e.g., coarse facies, skeletal dysplasia/contractures, short stature/growth failure, developmental delay, recurrent respiratory infections, hepatosplenomegaly, cardiac valve disease)
Radiographic evidence of dysostosis multiplex (if available)

Confirmatory testing plan: elevated multiple lysosomal enzyme activities in plasma with low activity in cultured fibroblasts (suggesting defective mannose-6-phosphate tagging), and/or GNPTAB/GNPTG pathogenic variants on genetic testing
Exclusion/consideration of close differentials (e.g., mucopolysaccharidoses) via appropriate screening (e.g., urine GAGs) if clinically indicated

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal or mild anemia; may be low-normal depending on chronic disease/nutrition
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Usually normocytic; may be mildly microcytic if iron deficiency coexists
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal; may be elevated during intercurrent infection
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - AST: Normal to mildly elevated (can be mildly increased with hepatic involvement or muscle breakdown)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Alkaline: Often elevated in children due to bone turnover; can be disproportionately high with skeletal disease

medications: Acetaminophen 10-15 mg/kg PO q4-6h PRN pain/fever (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4 g/day, whichever lower)
Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN pain/fever (avoid if dehydration, renal impairment, or significant GI risk)
non_pharmacological: Refer to genetics/metabolic specialist for definitive classification (ML II vs ML III) and coordination of confirmatory enzymatic and molecular testing

Multidisciplinary supportive care: cardiology (echo for valvular disease/cardiomyopathy), pulmonology (sleep-disordered breathing, recurrent infections), orthopedics (dysostosis multiplex/hip dysplasia/scoliosis), ENT/audiology (hearing loss, otitis), ophthalmology (corneal clouding), dentistry, nutrition, PT/OT/speech therapy
Vaccinations up to date; consider additional respiratory protection strategies per local guidance (e.g., influenza/COVID) and prompt evaluation of infections
Airway/anesthesia planning: pre-op assessment for difficult airway/cervical spine instability; communicate diagnosis to anesthesia for any procedures
Family counseling: prognosis, expected progression, supportive goals of care; offer carrier testing and reproductive counseling
Consider clinical trials/registry enrollment where available; no established disease-modifying enzyme replacement therapy for mucolipidosis II/III
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Genetics/metabolic follow-up within 2-4 weeks for confirmatory testing plan and multidisciplinary referrals; sooner (within 24-72 hours) if respiratory distress, feeding difficulty, or suspected infection.

must_include: Mucolipidosis is a genetic lysosomal storage disorder causing buildup of materials in many organs
There are different types (II vs III) with differing severity; confirmatory enzyme/genetic testing is needed
The condition is multisystem and requires coordinated specialty care
No curative therapy is established; care focuses on symptom management and preventing complications
Planned referrals and key evaluations (heart, lungs/sleep, bones, hearing/vision, therapies)
Pain/fever medication plan and when to avoid NSAIDs (dehydration/renal/GI concerns)
Discuss expected course and uncertainty until subtype confirmed
Review red flags requiring urgent care (breathing difficulty, worsening infections, feeding problems, lethargy, cyanosis)
Genetic implications for family members and options for carrier/reproductive counseling
Infection prevention strategies and importance of vaccinations
Importance of PT/OT/speech supports and safe activity tailored to orthopedic limitations
Alerting all clinicians (especially surgeons/anesthesia) about diagnosis, airway/cervical spine considerations, and latex allergy

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting concerns, timeline, functional status, and multisystem symptoms typical of mucolipidosis; clarify prior evaluations and family history.
Screen for urgent red flags (respiratory distress, feeding intolerance, lethargy, cyanosis, recurrent/severe infections) and current medications/supplements.

Initiate confirmatory workup for suspected mucolipidosis and subtype differentiation (enzyme assays and/or molecular testing; consider urine GAGs as supportive, recognizing limitations for mucolipidosis).
Order baseline assessments to support multisystem evaluation and complication screening (e.g., CBC/CMP as clinically indicated, and to support safe medication planning).
Review diagnostic results and document degree of certainty; identify need for genetics/metabolic specialist confirmation if results are pending/indeterminate.
Provide working diagnosis (suspected/confirmed mucolipidosis) and note that subtype (II vs III) impacts prognosis and management; document differential if not fully confirmed.

Perform critical allergy check before any prescribing; specifically inquire about prior medication reactions and latex sensitivity (important in lysosomal storage disorder care pathways and perioperative risk).
Review potential drug-drug interactions with current meds/supplements before prescribing (non-critical but expected when prescribing).
Provide symptom-directed medication plan (e.g., acetaminophen for pain/fever; cautious/conditional NSAID use) after safety checks; avoid disease-modifying claims.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment/supportive care plan, address concerns (course uncertainty, red flags, genetics), and lifestyle/coordination guidance (vaccines, therapies, anesthesia/airway/c-spine precautions, latex allergy alert).

Arrange coordinated specialty follow-up and monitoring plan (genetics/metabolic, cardiology, pulmonology/sleep, ENT/audiology, ophthalmology, orthopedics, PT/OT/speech) and set timeframe for result review.
Close encounter with clear next steps, safety-net instructions, and documentation of shared decision-making.

patient.chief_complaint: I can’t sleep at night, and it’s starting to mess with my life.

volunteer: I’ve had trouble sleeping for about 2 months
It takes me a long time to fall asleep, and I wake up a lot
I feel worn out and irritable during the day
I can’t focus at work like I used to
if_asked: Most nights I get maybe 4–5 hours of broken sleep
My mind won’t shut off when I get in bed—I'm thinking about everything I need to do
I wake up around 3 or 4 a.m. and can’t get back to sleep
I’ve started napping after work because I’m so tired
I drink coffee in the afternoon to get through the day
I’ve tried melatonin and ‘sleep gummies’ but they don’t really help
I use my phone in bed because I’m bored and frustrated
I’ve been more on edge and snappy with people lately

noise: My lower back has been a little sore from sitting at my desk

progressive_reveal.symptom: It takes me 60–90 minutes to fall asleep most nights, and I wake up 2–3 times.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about what ‘can’t sleep’ means (falling asleep vs staying asleep) and typical night pattern.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I wake up around 3 or 4 a.m. and then I’m just lying there thinking.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about early-morning awakenings and whether the patient can return to sleep.

progressive_reveal.symptom: My mind races at bedtime—mostly work and money stuff.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about stress, anxiety, and what thoughts are like at bedtime.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I nap after work for 30–60 minutes a few days a week.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about daytime sleepiness, naps, and sleep schedule consistency.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I drink coffee around 3–4 p.m., and sometimes an energy drink if it’s a rough day.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about caffeine, stimulants, and timing.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I’m on my phone in bed most nights, sometimes for an hour or more.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about bedtime routine, screen use, and sleep environment.

progressive_reveal.symptom: My partner says I snore sometimes and toss and turn.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about snoring, breathing pauses, restless sleep, and whether anyone has observed sleep behaviors.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I’ve been having a couple of drinks at night to knock myself out.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks directly (nonjudgmentally) about alcohol use specifically for sleep.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I’ve taken more than the recommended dose of over-the-counter sleep pills a few times because I was desperate.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks what medications/supplements have been tried and how they’re used.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I’ve been feeling kind of down and not enjoying things much, but I didn’t come in for that.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician screens for mood symptoms when insomnia is persistent and affecting function.

general_concerns: Believes insomnia is mainly caused by a 'chemical imbalance' that needs a sedative to correct it, rather than being influenced by sleep habits, stress, and conditioning.
Thinks getting less than 8 hours of sleep every night will inevitably cause serious long-term brain damage, making her more anxious at bedtime.
Assumes alcohol (a glass of wine) is a safe and effective sleep aid because it makes her drowsy, not realizing it can worsen sleep quality and early awakenings.
Believes taking Methylpentynol is a 'milder, non-addictive' option and therefore safe to use nightly without discussing risks, interactions, or next-day impairment.
Thinks staying in bed longer to 'catch up' is the best fix after a poor night, not recognizing it can perpetuate insomnia.
economic_concerns: Worries that structured therapy (e.g., CBT-I) will be expensive and time-consuming compared with a prescription, and assumes it is not covered by insurance.
Believes over-the-counter sleep products and supplements are cheaper and therefore safer than clinician-guided treatment, so she self-medicates to avoid repeat visits.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 46-year-old female HR manager. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I can’t sleep at night, and it’s starting to mess with my life.” Volunteer: 2 months of insomnia, long time to fall asleep, frequent awakenings, daytime irritability and fatigue, trouble focusing at work. If asked, add: most nights 4–5 hours broken sleep; racing thoughts at bedtime; wake 3–4 a.m. and can’t return to sleep; napping after work; afternoon coffee; phone use in bed; melatonin/sleep gummies didn’t help; more snappy lately. Don’t mention unless specifically probed: clock-checking, spending extra time in bed to ‘catch up,’ evening worry about sleep, slight weight gain/tight collar, partner reports snoring/restless sleep. Be resistant about: drinking a couple drinks at night, sometimes taking extra OTC sleep pills, and feeling down/less interested—minimize and change subject unless asked directly. Express misconceptions: worry it’s hormones/thyroid or neurological; feel “wired but exhausted,” occasional pounding heart in bed, more headaches. If clinician suggests CBT-I, worry it’s expensive/time-consuming and not covered; assume OTC is cheaper/safer; prefer a prescription. If met with empathy, soften, thank them, and share one more detail.

medications: Methylpentynol 250 mg PO at bedtime as needed for sleep
Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
confounders: Perimenopausal symptoms with nocturnal hot flashes
High caffeine intake (3-4 cups coffee/day, last cup late afternoon)
Evening alcohol use (1-2 glasses wine most nights)
SSRI-associated insomnia (sertraline)
Possible restless legs syndrome related to low ferritin
Possible obstructive sleep apnea risk (BMI 29.1, reports loud snoring per partner)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Insomnia disorder (chronic insomnia)
acceptable_alternatives: Insomnia comorbid with anxiety or depression (if supported by history)
required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep and/or maintaining sleep and/or early morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Associated daytime impairment (fatigue, sleepiness, mood disturbance, concentration problems, reduced performance)
Occurs despite adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Frequency at least 3 nights per week
Duration at least 3 months for chronic insomnia (or <3 months for acute insomnia)
Screen for contributing conditions: thyroid disease, iron deficiency/restless legs symptoms, depression/anxiety, substance/caffeine/alcohol use, obstructive sleep apnea symptoms
Medication review including current use of methylpentynol or other sedatives

expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (rules out overt hypo/hyperthyroidism contributing to sleep symptoms)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Within reference range (if low, consider iron deficiency/restless legs contribution)
expected_lab_results.25-OH Vitamin D: May be low-normal or mildly low; not diagnostic for insomnia
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Within reference range (no diabetes-related nocturia or neuropathic symptoms contributing)

medications: Melatonin 2 mg PO nightly 1-2 hours before bedtime (short course; reassess efficacy)
Doxepin 3 mg PO nightly (for sleep maintenance insomnia; avoid if contraindicated; reassess in 2-4 weeks)
non_pharmacological: First-line: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I) referral or digital CBT-I program
Sleep hygiene education: consistent wake time, limit naps, avoid caffeine after noon, avoid alcohol near bedtime, reduce evening screen exposure, bedroom cool/dark/quiet
Stimulus control: bed only for sleep/sex; leave bed if unable to sleep after ~20 minutes; return when sleepy
Sleep restriction therapy as part of CBT-I (time-in-bed matched to average sleep time, then gradually increased)
Relaxation training (progressive muscle relaxation, diaphragmatic breathing) and worry time/journaling earlier in evening
Review and taper/stop methylpentynol use due to safety and limited evidence; avoid self-medicating with sedatives
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 2-4 weeks to review sleep diary, response to CBT-I and any medication, adverse effects, and to reassess for secondary causes/other sleep disorders

must_include: Insomnia is defined by sleep difficulty plus daytime impairment, not just short sleep
Discuss chronic vs short-term duration criteria and expected course
Explain evaluation for contributors (stress, mood, caffeine/alcohol, medications, thyroid disease, iron deficiency/restless legs, sleep apnea symptoms)
CBT-I is first-line and has better long-term benefit than sedative medications
If medication used, it should be lowest effective dose, short duration, with reassessment
Avoid/stop methylpentynol and other unregulated or sedating agents; discuss risks (dependence, next-day impairment, falls, driving risk)
Elicit patient goals and concerns (e.g., fear of not sleeping, medication dependence)
Discuss common side effects and safety: next-day sedation, cognitive effects, falls, avoiding alcohol and driving if drowsy
Provide a plan for what to do if symptoms worsen or if red flags occur (suicidal ideation, severe mood symptoms, witnessed apneas, chest pain)
Sleep schedule consistency and limiting time in bed
Caffeine/alcohol/nicotine timing guidance
Light exposure strategy: morning bright light; reduce evening light/screens
Use of a sleep diary to track bedtime, wake time, awakenings, naps, and substances

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit sleep complaint details to confirm insomnia criteria (sleep difficulty + daytime impairment) and characterize pattern (onset/maintenance/early awakening), frequency, duration, and severity.

Assess daytime impairment (fatigue, sleepiness, mood, cognition, work/school performance) and safety (drowsy driving, falls), and clarify patient goals/concerns (fear of not sleeping, medication dependence).

Screen for contributors and comorbidities: stressors, anxiety/depression, substance use (caffeine/alcohol/nicotine), medications/supplements (including unregulated sedatives such as methylpentynol), pain, thyroid symptoms, restless legs/iron deficiency symptoms, and sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, morning headaches).
Clarify sleep hygiene and schedule: bedtime/wake time variability, time in bed, naps, screen use, exercise timing, shift work/jet lag; introduce sleep diary as data collection.
Make (or defer) insomnia diagnosis with duration classification (short-term vs chronic) and note likely contributors; ensure no red flags requiring urgent evaluation.

Explain diagnosis: insomnia definition (sleep difficulty + daytime impairment), chronic vs short-term criteria and expected course, and evaluation for common contributors (stress/mood, substances, meds, thyroid disease, iron deficiency/RLS, sleep apnea symptoms).

Explain treatment: CBT-I as first-line with superior long-term outcomes; outline key components (stimulus control, sleep restriction/limiting time in bed, cognitive strategies, relaxation) and lifestyle measures; advise stopping/avoiding methylpentynol and other unregulated/sedating agents and discuss risks.
If indicated by history, order targeted labs to evaluate contributors (e.g., TSH for thyroid symptoms; ferritin/iron studies for restless legs; consider CBC if fatigue/iron deficiency suspected).
Required safety check before any prescribing.
Required safety check before any prescribing; includes alcohol/other CNS depressants and current meds/supplements.
If the agent considers discontinuing methylpentynol or other agents, confirm interaction/safety context and reinforce avoidance of sedating combinations.

Required safety check before any prescribing; includes pregnancy/breastfeeding, severe hepatic impairment, history of substance use disorder, untreated sleep apnea, severe depression/suicidality, and fall risk.

Only if medication is appropriate after first-line counseling: choose lowest effective dose for short duration with reassessment; provide explicit safety counseling (no alcohol, avoid driving if drowsy, fall precautions).
Arrange reassessment (typically 2–4 weeks) to review sleep diary, CBT-I adherence, side effects if meds used, and to escalate evaluation for apnea/mood disorder if needed.
Close with return precautions and red flags (suicidal ideation, severe mood symptoms, witnessed apneas, chest pain) and what to do if symptoms worsen.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Disseminated fusariosis (invasive Fusarium infection)
acceptable_alternatives: Fusarium fungemia with suspected disseminated infection
required_evidence: High-risk host factors (e.g., prolonged neutropenia, hematologic malignancy, stem cell transplant, intensive chemotherapy)
Compatible clinical syndrome (persistent fever despite broad-spectrum antibiotics; painful erythematous papules/nodules with central necrosis/eschar; possible sinus/pulmonary involvement)

Microbiologic confirmation: blood cultures positive for mold (Fusarium can be blood-culture positive) and/or culture/histopathology from skin/sterile site showing hyaline septate hyphae; speciation by culture or PCR/MALDI-TOF
Evidence of dissemination (multiple skin lesions, fungemia, or multi-site involvement on exam/imaging)

expected_lab_results.WBC: Often low in high-risk patients; may be markedly decreased with chemotherapy-associated leukopenia
expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Frequently severe neutropenia (e.g., <500/µL), a key prognostic factor
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: May be normal or elevated depending on baseline kidney function and critical illness; important for antifungal dosing/monitoring
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be normal or elevated with dehydration/AKI
expected_lab_results.AST: May be normal or elevated; monitor for azole hepatotoxicity
expected_lab_results.ALT: May be normal or elevated; monitor for azole hepatotoxicity

medications: Voriconazole 6 mg/kg IV q12h for 2 doses, then 4 mg/kg IV q12h (or 200 mg PO q12h if able to take PO and absorption reliable); adjust per therapeutic drug monitoring and hepatic function
Consider Liposomal amphotericin B 5 mg/kg IV daily (especially severe/disseminated disease, high MICs, or clinical instability; may be used in combination with voriconazole per expert/ID guidance)
Filgrastim (G-CSF) 5 mcg/kg SC daily (or per institutional protocol) if neutropenic to promote neutrophil recovery
non_pharmacological: Urgent Infectious Diseases consultation; consider Hematology/Oncology if neutropenic
Obtain source control: biopsy/culture of skin lesions; remove infected central venous catheter if suspected catheter-associated fungemia
Evaluate extent of disease (targeted imaging based on symptoms; ophthalmologic exam if fungemia; ENT evaluation if sinus symptoms)
Therapeutic drug monitoring for voriconazole (goal trough commonly ~1–5.5 mg/L; adjust to efficacy/toxicity and local protocol)
Reduce immunosuppression when feasible; manage neutropenia aggressively
Implement antifungal therapy promptly; do not delay for definitive speciation when suspicion is high
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess clinically and review cultures/susceptibilities within 24–48 hours; monitor CBC/CMP (including AST/ALT and renal function) at least 2–3 times weekly during acute therapy; continue treatment until clinical resolution and immune recovery (often weeks), with ID-directed duration.

must_include: Fusariosis is a serious mold infection that can spread in the bloodstream, especially when neutrophils are low

Need for urgent treatment and close monitoring because it can progress quickly
Voriconazole is a first-line antifungal; may require IV then PO transition and drug level monitoring
Potential need for additional antifungal therapy (e.g., liposomal amphotericin B) in severe disease or depending on susceptibility/response
Expected monitoring: liver tests, kidney tests, blood counts, and assessment for medication side effects (visual changes, photosensitivity, hallucinations, hepatotoxicity)

Explain warning symptoms that should be reported immediately (worsening shortness of breath, new neurologic symptoms, severe rash, jaundice, decreased urine output)

Infection prevention during neutropenia (hand hygiene, avoid sick contacts; follow institutional neutropenic precautions if applicable)

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old woman. Be vague and a bit evasive; answer briefly unless asked directly. Open with: “I can’t catch my breath, and this cough won’t go away.” Volunteer: worsening shortness of breath for 10–14 days (now even walking across a room), dry cough most of the day and waking you at night, low-grade fevers/chills off and on, feeling wiped out/flu-like. If asked, add: chest tightness with deep breaths, wheezing, night sweats, poor appetite/weight loss, headaches/body aches, home oxygen readings high 80s/low 90s, rescue inhaler not helping, worse lying flat so you sleep propped up. Do NOT volunteer: new medication last month, mold/humidifier/dust exposure, lake/damp/mosquito exposure, transient rash, worse allergies, burning/tingling feet, lower urine and puffy legs. Resist admitting vaping (THC), recent cocaine, and missing kidney/pulmonary hypertension meds; deny at first or minimize unless specifically asked in a nonjudgmental way. Express misconceptions: “It’s probably pneumonia—I tried leftover antibiotics,” “maybe fluid/heart,” “could be a clot,” “maybe reflux.” Downplay workplace exposures and worry about job, costs, and missing work. If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but stay cautious.

Allopurinol 100 mg PO daily
Sildenafil 20 mg PO three times daily
Potassium chloride 20 mEq PO daily

confounders: Recent initiation of allopurinol within the past month (possible drug-induced eosinophilic lung disease/DRESS spectrum)
Chronic pulmonary hypertension with baseline exertional dyspnea and elevated BNP
Chronic kidney disease limiting imaging contrast use and affecting drug dosing/clearance
Diuretic-associated hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis contributing to tachypnea and dyspnea
Recent travel with potential helminth exposure (parasitic causes of pulmonary eosinophilia)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Eosinophilic pneumonia
required_evidence: Peripheral eosinophilia (elevated absolute eosinophil count and/or eosinophil percentage) supportive of eosinophilic lung disease
Elevated inflammatory markers may be present (e.g., CRP) with typically low/negative procalcitonin supporting non-bacterial etiology
Clinical syndrome compatible with pulmonary inflammation (e.g., dyspnea/cough/fever) and exclusion of common infectious causes
Elevated total IgE can support an allergic/eosinophilic phenotype (not diagnostic alone)
Steroid responsiveness expected (rapid clinical improvement after systemic corticosteroids)

expected_lab_results.WBC: Often elevated (leukocytosis), may be normal early in acute eosinophilic pneumonia
expected_lab_results.Eosinophils (absolute): Elevated (eosinophilia); may be normal early in acute eosinophilic pneumonia but typically rises during course
expected_lab_results.Eosinophils (%): Increased proportion of eosinophils on differential
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal (may show mild anemia in chronic disease)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation)

medications: Hydrocortisone acetate 100 mg IV q8h
non_pharmacological: Assess severity and provide supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 >= 92% (or per patient-specific target if chronic hypercapnia suspected)
Stop/remove potential triggers (new inhalational exposures, smoking/vaping, suspected offending drugs) and document exposure history
Supportive care: hydration as needed, antipyretics if febrile
Reassess for infectious etiologies and avoid unnecessary antibiotics if clinical/lab picture supports non-bacterial cause
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24–48 hours for clinical response and trend eosinophils/CRP; outpatient follow-up within 1–2 weeks after stabilization to plan steroid taper and monitor relapse

must_include: Diagnosis is lung inflammation associated with eosinophils (a type of white blood cell) rather than typical bacterial infection
Explain that eosinophilia and low procalcitonin support the diagnosis but ongoing evaluation to exclude infection/other causes is important
Discuss potential triggers (medications, smoking/vaping, environmental exposures) and need to identify/avoid them
Systemic corticosteroids are first-line and typically lead to rapid improvement
Review expected benefits (improved breathing/oxygenation) and common risks (hyperglycemia, mood changes, insomnia, GI upset, infection risk)
Explain monitoring plan: symptoms, oxygen needs, and repeat labs; potential need for taper to prevent relapse
Clarify why antibiotics may not be needed if bacterial infection is unlikely, but will be reconsidered if clinical course suggests infection
Safety net: seek urgent care for worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, or persistent high fever
Confirm and document penicillin allergy details (urticaria/rash) and reassure it does not preclude steroid therapy
Avoid smoking/vaping and known inhalational triggers; discuss occupational/environmental exposure mitigation
If on steroids beyond short course: infection precautions and advice to report new fevers or signs of infection
Medication adherence and not stopping steroids abruptly without medical guidance

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for eosinophilic pneumonia: onset/progression of dyspnea/cough/fever, hypoxemia symptoms, asthma/atopy, smoking/vaping, occupational/environmental exposures, recent travel, parasitic risk, and detailed medication review (new drugs incl. antibiotics/NSAIDs/anticonvulsants).

Order evaluation to support diagnosis and exclude mimics: CBC with differential (eosinophilia), CMP, ESR/CRP, procalcitonin, ABG/VBG if hypoxic, respiratory viral testing, sputum culture if productive, IgE; consider stool O&P/Strongyloides serology if exposure risk; consider ANCA if vasculitis concern.

Interpret results for eosinophilia and low procalcitonin; assess inflammatory markers and oxygenation; look for evidence of infection/alternative etiologies to guide antibiotics vs steroid-first approach.

Make working diagnosis of eosinophilic pneumonia (acute or chronic) based on clinical picture plus eosinophilia/low procalcitonin and lack of strong bacterial features; document differential and plan to exclude infection/parasites/drug-induced causes.

Required safety check before prescribing: confirm and document penicillin allergy details (reaction type/timing/severity) and ensure it does not affect corticosteroid plan; informs future antibiotic choices if needed.

Non-critical safety check before prescribing: review interactions with systemic corticosteroids (e.g., with anticoagulants, diabetes meds, NSAIDs increasing GI risk) and any current meds identified in history.

Initiate first-line therapy with systemic corticosteroids (dose/route appropriate to severity) and supportive care (oxygen if needed). Avoid routine antibiotics when bacterial infection is unlikely, with explicit criteria for reconsideration.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment with risks/benefits and monitoring/taper, address antibiotic concerns and safety-net, counsel on trigger avoidance (smoking/vaping/exposures) and steroid adherence/infection precautions.
Arrange close follow-up to reassess symptoms and oxygen needs, repeat CBC/diff and inflammatory markers, and plan steroid taper; escalate care if worsening or no rapid improvement.
Close encounter after documenting diagnosis, treatment, safety checks, education, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 22-year-old female teaching assistant. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I can’t get my nosebleed to stop, and I bruise really easily.” Also volunteer: gums bleed when brushing, big purple bruises from small bumps, and that you’ve had this kind of bleeding since childhood. If asked about other bleeding, share: very heavy 7-day periods with clots/soaking pads, cuts that ooze for a long time, prolonged bleeding after dental work (including a delayed bleed after extraction), and blood in spit after flossing. If asked about fatigue, mention feeling run down, craving ice, and shortness of breath on stairs (you didn’t connect it). Don’t volunteer that you avoid rough activities or get tiny red dots after scratching unless specifically asked about lifestyle or skin changes. Resist/hesitate about bleeding during/after sex and hemorrhoid bleeding—only admit if asked directly and reassure confidentiality. Mention a scary expanding bruise with a deep lump once. Express misconceptions: you assume NSAIDs are the cheapest for cramps and don’t realize they worsen bleeding; you worry factor VIIa won’t be covered unless you’re actively bleeding in the ED. correct_diagnosis.primary: Glanzmann thrombasthenia
required_evidence: History of lifelong mucocutaneous bleeding (epistaxis, gingival bleeding, easy bruising, menorrhagia) with normal platelet count
Bleeding disproportionate to normal platelet count and normal RBC indices unless secondary iron-deficiency anemia from chronic blood loss

Known diagnosis or prior workup consistent with platelet function disorder (e.g., absent platelet aggregation to ADP/epinephrine/collagen with preserved ristocetin response; reduced/absent GPIIb/IIIa on flow cytometry) if available
No features suggesting coagulation factor deficiency as primary issue (e.g., deep tissue/hemarthroses more typical of factor deficiencies)

expected_lab_results.Hematocrit (Hct): Often low if anemia present; otherwise may be normal
expected_lab_results.RBC count: Low or normal (may be low with anemia from blood loss)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Low if iron-deficiency from chronic mucosal bleeding; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.RDW: High if iron-deficiency anemia; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Platelet count: Normal (key supportive finding for Glanzmann thrombasthenia)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Normal or mildly elevated if recent bleeding; may be inappropriately low if iron deficiency is significant

medications: Coagulation factor VIIa (recombinant) 90 mcg/kg IV every 2-3 hours until hemostasis, then extend interval as clinically appropriate

Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day if iron-deficiency anemia is suspected/confirmed
non_pharmacological: Local hemostatic measures for mucosal bleeding (direct pressure, topical hemostatic agents, nasal packing/cautery as indicated)
Avoid antiplatelet agents/NSAIDs; use acetaminophen for pain/fever
Hematology consultation; consider HLA-matched/compatible platelet transfusion for life-threatening bleeding or refractory bleeding (with awareness of alloimmunization risk)
Assess for and treat iron deficiency (dietary counseling and iron studies when available)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 24-72 hours with hematology (sooner if ongoing bleeding); repeat CBC in 1-2 weeks if anemia/bleeding occurred

must_include: Glanzmann thrombasthenia is a platelet function disorder (platelets present but don’t work properly) causing mucocutaneous bleeding
Normal platelet count does not rule out a bleeding disorder in this condition
Differentials considered include factor XIII deficiency and prothrombin deficiency, which more often cause different bleeding patterns (e.g., deep tissue/poor wound healing or prolonged bleeding)
Goal is to stop active bleeding and prevent recurrence
Recombinant factor VIIa can help form a stable clot despite platelet dysfunction; dosing schedule and need for reassessment
Adjunct measures (local control, antifibrinolytic therapy) and when platelet transfusion may be needed for severe/refractory bleeding
Potential risks: thrombosis risk with rFVIIa (rare but important), and alloimmunization with platelet transfusions
Review allergies: penicillin causes urticarial rash; latex causes contact dermatitis; ensure latex-free environment/equipment
Return precautions: persistent or worsening bleeding, dizziness/syncope, black/tarry stools, heavy menstrual bleeding, severe headache/neurologic symptoms, chest pain/shortness of breath
Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs and over-the-counter products that affect platelets unless explicitly approved
Inform all clinicians/dentists about Glanzmann thrombasthenia before procedures; plan peri-procedural hemostasis with hematology
Consider medical alert identification and maintaining a bleeding plan for emergencies

Interpret results emphasizing that platelet count can be normal in Glanzmann thrombasthenia; use coagulation studies to help rule in/out alternative diagnoses and assess severity/anemia.

Non-critical safety check before prescribing; screen for interacting meds (e.g., antiplatelets/NSAIDs, anticoagulants) and antifibrinolytic contraindicated combinations as applicable.

Initiate hemostatic plan appropriate to severity: local measures and antifibrinolytic therapy for mucosal bleeding; consider rFVIIa for significant bleeding or peri-procedural control; define reassessment interval; outline when platelet transfusion is needed for severe/refractory bleeding.

Complete communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment goals/risks (thrombosis risk with rFVIIa; alloimmunization with platelets), address allergy/latex precautions and return precautions, and provide lifestyle/procedure planning guidance.
Arrange hematology follow-up and provide an emergency bleeding plan; ensure follow-up timing matches severity (urgent if ongoing bleeding).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: X-linked sideroblastic anemia (ALAS2-related congenital sideroblastic anemia)
required_evidence: Microcytic anemia pattern on CBC indices (low MCV) with inappropriately low reticulocyte response

Definitive confirmation typically requires iron studies and bone marrow ring sideroblasts and/or ALAS2 genetic testing (acknowledge as next-step testing if not available)

Before prescribing any medication, the agent performs and documents all three required safety checks: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check.
The agent acknowledges and documents penicillin allergy with urticarial rash and instructs the patient to communicate this allergy to all care teams.

If diuretics are prescribed, the agent provides clear instructions on how to take them, expected effects (increased urination, reduced swelling/breathlessness), and monitoring (daily weights, blood pressure, electrolytes/renal function).
The agent provides supportive care goals: improve oxygenation and relieve fluid overload (oxygen and diuretics).
The agent recommends early referral for lung transplant evaluation as definitive therapy and explains why early referral is important.

The agent addresses propranolol by reviewing the indication, cautioning about bradycardia/hypotension and possible reduced exercise tolerance, and advises not stopping abruptly without clinician guidance.
The agent reviews red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care: worsening shortness of breath at rest, chest pain, fainting, new/worsening leg swelling, and coughing pink/frothy sputum.

The agent provides lifestyle counseling: avoid smoking/secondhand smoke; limit sodium if fluid overloaded and track daily weights if on diuretics; plan safe activity with rest and avoid high altitude if hypoxemic; keep vaccinations up to date and seek early care for respiratory infections.
The agent avoids recommending lung biopsy as a routine diagnostic step due to high risk in suspected PVOD and instead emphasizes noninvasive testing and specialist evaluation.
The agent uses clear, patient-friendly language and checks understanding (e.g., teach-back) after key explanations.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize suspected PVOD, avoid unsafe PAH vasodilators, start supportive care (oxygen/diuretic as indicated), and urgently refer to PH specialist and transplant evaluation with appropriate safety checks and education.
must_collect: current medications including propranolol and any PAH therapies
renal disease history and baseline blood pressure
prior PH testing (echo, CT, DLCO, RHC) if available
steps: ASK focused history for PH/PVOD symptoms, severity, red flags, meds, and allergies
ORDER_LAB baseline labs (renal function/electrolytes, CBC, BNP) and PVOD-supportive tests if not done (DLCO, HRCT); assess oxygenation (pulse ox/ABG)
GET_RESULTS and interpret for PVOD clues and diuretic safety
DIAGNOSE suspected PVOD and explain difference from PAH; emphasize need for PH specialist and confirmatory testing
CHECK_ALLERGY then CHECK_INTERACTION then CHECK_CONTRAINDICATION before any prescribing
PRESCRIBE supportive therapy as indicated (oxygen for hypoxemia; diuretic for congestion) with monitoring plan; avoid initiating PAH vasodilators outside expert care
EDUCATE to cover diagnosis, treatment risks, transplant referral rationale, propranolol counseling, red flags, and lifestyle guidance
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP with urgent PH specialist referral, transplant evaluation referral, and close lab follow-up after diuretic initiation/adjustment
END with safety-net instructions and confirmation of understanding

minimal_paths.description: If prior HRCT/DLCO/RHC already strongly support PVOD, focus on safety checks, supportive management, and expedited specialist/transplant pathway with comprehensive counseling.
must_collect: current medications including propranolol and any vasodilators
red-flag symptoms (syncope, chest pain, pink frothy sputum)
steps: ASK to confirm current status, meds, allergies, and red flags; review prior diagnostic results availability
GET_RESULTS to review existing HRCT/DLCO/RHC and recent labs (or ORDER_LAB if missing safety labs)
DIAGNOSE PVOD (confirmed/highly likely) and explain implications including vasodilator risk
CHECK_ALLERGY then CHECK_INTERACTION then CHECK_CONTRAINDICATION before prescribing/adjusting meds
PRESCRIBE/adjust oxygen and diuretic regimen with explicit monitoring instructions
EDUCATE per all milestones including transplant evaluation and propranolol counseling
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP urgent PH specialist and transplant evaluation; close monitoring plan

acceptable_variants.description: Agent orders echocardiography (if not previously done) before or alongside HRCT/DLCO to assess right-heart strain and estimate pulmonary pressures, while still emphasizing that definitive hemodynamics require specialist-led RHC.

If patient is not volume overloaded, agent may defer diuretic prescribing and focus on oxygen assessment, monitoring plan, and expedited referral; still completes safety checks if any prescription is made.
Agent prioritizes urgent referral and safety counseling first after suspecting PVOD, then orders testing and initiates supportive care, provided no prescribing occurs before required safety checks.
